text: The speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT) is one of the most ubiquitous phenomena in behavioral science. It is observed in almost every species, from insects and rodents to primates. Recently, advances in neuroscience are starting to shed light on its neural substrates, and many computational models use the SAT as a gold standard for decision-making. Here, we give a historical account of the evolution of models of decision-making in the context of the SAT, and then provide a 'user’s guide' to studying the SAT, with a critical discussion of the different manipulations and techniques that have been employed to study this phenomenon, as well as the behavioral patterns that emerge when the decision-maker is directly in control of the SAT. Finally, we discuss the applications of these techniques to the study of decision-making.
